content.text: Creating Management Groups

Management groups are created beneath the Root Management Group to segment the Azure tenant into governance zones. Each group is a container for subscriptions and other management groups. Creating the right hierarchy upfront enables you to apply policies and role assignments at the correct granularity and reduces the number of individual assignments needed as the tenant grows.

attrs.alt: CAF landing zone management group structure with Platform, Corp, Online, and Sandbox top-level groups

content.text: Prerequisites and Permissions

To create a management group you need one of these roles at the parent scope: Management Group Contributor, Management Group Owner, or Owner. If the management groups feature has not been enabled for the tenant, a Global Administrator must enable it first.

# Enable management groups for the tenant (first time only, requires Global Admin)
az account management-group create --name 'tenant-root-group' 2>/dev/null || true

# Verify the Root Management Group exists
TENANT_ID=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)
az account management-group show --name $TENANT_ID --query '{Name:name, DisplayName:properties.displayName}'
Creating a Management Group via CLI

The `az account management-group create` command creates a management group and optionally sets its display name and parent. If `--parent` is omitted, the group is created directly under the Root Management Group.
Basic creation

TENANT_ID=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)

# Create a top-level group under root
az account management-group create \
  --name 'mg-corp' \
  --display-name 'Corp (Regulated Workloads)' \
  --parent $TENANT_ID

# Create a nested group under mg-corp
az account management-group create \
  --name 'mg-corp-prod' \
  --display-name 'Corp - Production' \
  --parent 'mg-corp'

az account management-group create \
  --name 'mg-corp-nonprod' \
  --display-name 'Corp - Non-Production' \
  --parent 'mg-corp'

# Verify the hierarchy
az account management-group show \
  --name 'mg-corp' \
  --expand \
  --query '{Name:name, Children:children[].displayName}'
Naming conventions

Management group names (IDs) must be 1–90 characters, alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores, and parentheses only. The name is immutable after creation — the display name can be changed but the `--name` ID cannot. Use a prefix like `mg-` to distinguish management group names from subscription names in CLI output.
Good name
Display name
Purpose
mg-platform
Platform
Shared infrastructure: hub networking, identity, log analytics
mg-corp
Corp (Regulated)
Production workloads with compliance requirements
mg-corp-prod
Corp - Production
Production subscriptions under Corp
mg-corp-nonprod
Corp - Non-Production
Dev/test subscriptions under Corp
mg-online
Online (Internet-Facing)
Public-facing workloads with different policy set
mg-sandbox
Sandbox
Developer experimentation; relaxed policy set
mg-decommissioned
Decommissioned
Subscriptions being wound down; deny new resources
Creating the Full CAF Landing Zone Hierarchy
The Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) recommends a well-known starting structure. The following script creates the full top-level hierarchy and the Platform sub-groups in a single run.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
TENANT_ID=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)

create_mg() {
  local name=$1 display=$2 parent=$3
  if az account management-group show --name "$name" &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Already exists: $name"
  else
    az account management-group create --name "$name" --display-name "$display" --parent "$parent"
    echo "Created: $name ($display)"
  fi
}

# Top-level groups
create_mg 'mg-platform'      'Platform'               $TENANT_ID
create_mg 'mg-corp'          'Corp (Regulated)'       $TENANT_ID
create_mg 'mg-online'        'Online'                 $TENANT_ID
create_mg 'mg-sandbox'       'Sandbox'                $TENANT_ID
create_mg 'mg-decommissioned' 'Decommissioned'        $TENANT_ID

# Platform sub-groups
create_mg 'mg-platform-connectivity' 'Connectivity'   'mg-platform'
create_mg 'mg-platform-identity'     'Identity'       'mg-platform'
create_mg 'mg-platform-management'   'Management'     'mg-platform'

# Corp sub-groups
create_mg 'mg-corp-prod'    'Corp - Production'       'mg-corp'
create_mg 'mg-corp-nonprod' 'Corp - Non-Production'   'mg-corp'

echo 'Hierarchy created.'
az account management-group list --query '[].{Name:name, Parent:details.parent.id}' -o table
Creating Management Groups via Terraform

Terraform's `azurerm_management_group` resource manages management groups idempotently. Changes to display names or parent relationships are applied on the next `terraform apply`. The group name (ID) is immutable: changing `name` destroys and re-creates the group, which removes all policy and RBAC assignments.

Managing management groups in Terraform means that new groups created outside of IaC will be detected as drift and removed on the next apply. Decide upfront whether Terraform owns the hierarchy exclusively or acts as a partial overlay.
Creating Management Groups via Bicep (Tenant Scope)
Moving and Deleting Management Groups

A management group's parent can be changed to restructure the hierarchy. A group can only be deleted if it contains no subscriptions and no child management groups. Before deleting, move all subscriptions out and delete or move all child groups.

# Move a management group to a new parent
az account management-group update \
  --name 'mg-corp-nonprod' \
  --parent 'mg-sandbox'  # Move Non-Production under Sandbox MG

# Delete a management group (must be empty)
az account management-group delete --name 'mg-decommissioned'

# List subscriptions in a group before deleting
az account management-group show \
  --name 'mg-decommissioned' \
  --expand \
  --query 'children[?type==`/subscriptions`].displayName'
The Root Management Group cannot be deleted or moved.
Deleting a management group does not affect subscriptions inside it — they remain in the tenant and fall directly under the parent of the deleted group.
Policy assignments and RBAC assignments at the deleted group's scope are also deleted when the group is removed.
If you rename a management group (change display name), the ID (name) stays the same and all policy/RBAC assignments are unaffected.
Limits and Constraints
Constraint
Limit
Maximum management groups per tenant
Maximum tree depth (below root)
6 levels
Maximum name length
90 characters
Allowed characters in name
Alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores, parentheses
Can a subscription be in multiple MGs?
No — one MG parent at a time
Can the name be changed after creation?
No — only the display name can change
Who can create a top-level MG?
Owner or Management Group Contributor at root scope
Automating Management Group Creation in Azure DevOps Pipelines

Organisations that use Azure DevOps for infrastructure automation can run the management group creation script as part of a pipeline triggered from a `main` branch push. This ensures that the hierarchy in IaC is always the authoritative source and that manual portal changes are visible as drift.

# azure-pipelines-mg-hierarchy.yml
trigger:
  branches:
    include:
      - main
  paths:
    include:
      - governance/management-groups/**

pool:
  vmImage: ubuntu-latest

variables:
  - group: governance-secrets  # Contains ARM_TENANT_ID, service connection name

stages:
  - stage: Plan
    jobs:
      - job: TerraformPlan
        steps:
          - task: TerraformTaskV4@4
            inputs:
              provider: azurerm
              command: init
              workingDirectory: governance/management-groups
              backendServiceArm: 'governance-service-connection'
              backendAzureRmResourceGroupName: 'rg-tfstate'
              backendAzureRmStorageAccountName: 'satfstategov'
              backendAzureRmContainerName: 'tfstate'
              backendAzureRmKey: 'management-groups.tfstate'
          - task: TerraformTaskV4@4
            inputs:
              provider: azurerm
              command: plan
              workingDirectory: governance/management-groups
              environmentServiceNameAzureRM: 'governance-service-connection'
              commandOptions: '-out=mgplan.tfplan'

  - stage: Apply
    dependsOn: Plan
    condition: and(succeeded(), eq(variables['Build.SourceBranch'], 'refs/heads/main'))
    jobs:
      - deployment: ApplyMGHierarchy
        environment: 'governance-prod'  # Requires manual approval gate
        strategy:
          runOnce:
            deploy:
              steps:
                - task: TerraformTaskV4@4
                  inputs:
                    provider: azurerm
                    command: apply
                    workingDirectory: governance/management-groups
                    environmentServiceNameAzureRM: 'governance-service-connection'
                    commandOptions: 'mgplan.tfplan'

The pipeline uses a deployment job with an Environment approval gate before `terraform apply`. This means a human reviewer must approve any changes to the management group hierarchy — protecting against accidental restructuring that would change policy inheritance across the entire tenant.
Verifying the Hierarchy with Azure Resource Graph

After creating the management group structure, use Azure Resource Graph to verify the full hierarchy without having to traverse each group individually. Resource Graph aggregates management group data from all linked subscriptions.

# Query the full management group tree
az graph query -q "
  ResourceContainers
  | where type == 'microsoft.management/managementgroups'
  | extend ParentId = tostring(properties.details.parent.id)
  | project Name=name, DisplayName=properties.displayName, ParentId
  | order by Name asc
" --query data -o table

# Find all subscriptions and which MG they belong to
az graph query -q "
  ResourceContainers
  | where type == 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
  | extend MGParent = tostring(properties.managementGroupAncestorsChain[0].name)
  | project SubName=name, SubscriptionId=subscriptionId, DirectParentMG=MGParent
  | order by DirectParentMG asc
" --query data -o table

# Count subscriptions per management group
az graph query -q "
  ResourceContainers
  | where type == 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
  | extend MGParent = tostring(properties.managementGroupAncestorsChain[0].name)
  | summarize SubCount=count() by MGParent
  | order by SubCount desc
" --query data -o table
Policy Assignments Created Alongside the Hierarchy

Management groups are most valuable when policy is assigned at the same time as the group is created. The following pattern assigns a deny policy for non-approved regions to the Corp group at creation time, ensuring that any subscription placed under Corp immediately inherits the regional restriction.

# Create the Corp management group
az account management-group create \
  --name 'mg-corp' \
  --display-name 'Corp (Regulated)' \
  --parent $TENANT_ID

CORP_SCOPE='/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp'

# Find the Allowed Locations built-in policy definition ID
ALLOWED_LOC_POLICY=$(az policy definition list \
  --query "[?displayName=='Allowed locations'].id" -o tsv | head -1)

# Assign at Corp scope: only eastus and westeurope are allowed
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'allowed-regions-corp' \
  --display-name 'Restrict resources to approved regions (Corp)' \
  --policy $ALLOWED_LOC_POLICY \
  --scope $CORP_SCOPE \
  --params '{"listOfAllowedLocations":{"value":["eastus","eastus2","westeurope"]}}'

# Assign a deny policy for public blob access at Corp scope
DENY_PUBLIC_BLOB=$(az policy definition list \
  --query "[?displayName contains 'Deny public blob access'].id" -o tsv | head -1)

az policy assignment create \
  --name 'deny-public-blob-corp' \
  --display-name 'Deny public blob access (Corp)' \
  --policy $DENY_PUBLIC_BLOB \
  --scope $CORP_SCOPE

echo 'Corp management group created with regional restriction and public blob deny policies.'
Terraform: management group + policy in one module
Auditing Management Group Creation Events

The Azure Activity Log records every management group create, update, and delete operation. These events are tenant-scoped and appear under the `Microsoft.Management` resource provider. Querying them helps you detect unauthorised hierarchy changes.

// Detect new management group creation in the last 30 days
AzureActivity
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where ResourceProviderValue == 'MICROSOFT.MANAGEMENT'
| where OperationNameValue == 'MICROSOFT.MANAGEMENT/MANAGEMENTGROUPS/WRITE'
| where ActivityStatusValue == 'Success'
| extend MGName = tostring(todynamic(Properties).resource)
| project TimeGenerated, MGName, Caller, SubscriptionId
| order by TimeGenerated desc

// Detect management group deletions or subscription moves
AzureActivity
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where ResourceProviderValue == 'MICROSOFT.MANAGEMENT'
| where OperationNameValue in (
    'MICROSOFT.MANAGEMENT/MANAGEMENTGROUPS/DELETE',
    'MICROSOFT.MANAGEMENT/MANAGEMENTGROUPS/SUBSCRIPTIONS/WRITE',
    'MICROSOFT.MANAGEMENT/MANAGEMENTGROUPS/SUBSCRIPTIONS/DELETE'
  )
| where ActivityStatusValue == 'Success'
| project TimeGenerated, Operation=OperationNameValue, Caller, Properties
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Summary

Management groups are created with `az account management-group create`, Terraform's `azurerm_management_group` resource, or a tenant-scoped Bicep template. The Cloud Adoption Framework landing zone structure — Platform, Corp, Online, Sandbox, Decommissioned — provides a practical starting point for organising governance zones. Key constraints: the group name is immutable after creation, a group must be empty before it can be deleted, and the hierarchy can be at most six levels deep below the root. Manage the hierarchy in IaC from the start to prevent drift and make structural changes auditable.
